interactions.summary: The host introduces Jiang as the figure behind Predictive History and asks how the lectures began.

Jiang narrates his path from wide curiosity, Yale alienation, law-school rejection, return to China, deportation during a PBS project, Starcraft depression, and Afghanistan UN work, where he says seeing empire and NGO corruption changed his understanding of the world.

speaker_notes.note: Diarization cleanly separates host introduction from Jiang's long autobiographical answer.

claims.claim: Jiang frames his intellectual origin as curiosity rather than career ambition, saying science fiction, comics, and Yale's course breadth trained his wide-ranging habits.
claims.temporal_scope: Autobiographical origin story before Predictive History.

claims.claim: Jiang says Yale's elite social world treated people instrumentally, leaving him alienated because he did not fit the ambition-and-usefulness culture.
claims.temporal_scope: Retrospective account of Jiang's Yale years.

claims.claim: Jiang identifies his Afghanistan UN work as a rude awakening to American empire, NGO corruption, warlord protection, relic smuggling, opium protection, and expat profiteering.
claims.temporal_scope: Retrospective account of Afghanistan experience before Predictive History.

signature_moments.moment: Afghanistan becomes the experiential break where the abstract idea of empire turns into a visible scam.
signature_moments.source_phrase: first exposure actually to the American empire overseas
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It explains why the later Predictive History world model begins from institutional distrust rather than detached theory.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: Jiang's name for the operational system he says he saw in Afghanistan: military, NGO, UN, warlord, and illicit-economy arrangements reinforcing each other.

chronology_notes.note: Jiang places his China journalism arrest/deportation before the Starcraft depression period and before his six-month UN Afghanistan exposure.

uncertainty_notes.note: ASR phrase 'higher education' may refer to a publication or outlet; keep as transcript wording unless checked against audio.
